avalancheActivity.highlights: Some snow will fall during the night in some regions. The backcountry touring conditions remain generally favourable.
avalancheActivity.comment:
The sometimes strong wind will transport the new snow. The fresh wind slabs represent the main danger. They are easy to recognise but to be assessed with care and prudence.

Individual small moist snow slides are possible as the day progresses, in the event of prolonged bright spells in particular on extremely steep sunny slopes.
Weak layers in the old snowpack can be released in isolated cases. These avalanche prone locations are to be found in particular on very steep shady slopes above approximately 2500 m and at transitions from a shallow to a deep snowpack.

snowpackStructure.comment:
In some regions 2 to 10 cm of snow fell on Tuesday above approximately 2200 m. This snow will be deposited on surface hoar in particular on shady slopes.
Towards its surface, the snowpack is largely stable and its surface has a melt-freeze crust that is strong in many cases.
Especially steep shady slopes high altitudes and the high Alpine regions: Snow that is very compacted on the surface and stabilised by the stormy winds at the end of February, or composed of faceted crystals and, locally, snow with weak cohesion and surface frost. Weak layers exist in the old snowpack here.
Adjacent to ridgelines and in pass areas as well as at high altitude snow depths vary greatly, depending on the infuence of the wind.
In some regions 5 to 15 cm of snow, and up to 20 cm in some localities, will fall during the night above approximately 2200 m.

tendency.highlights: The weather will be sunny. Slight increase in danger of moist and wet avalanches as a consequence of warming during the day and solar radiation. This applies on very steep sunny slopes below approximately 2700 m.

avalancheActivity.comment: Over a wide area light precipitation. Moist and wet avalanches require caution. The avalanche prone locations are to be found in particular at the base of rock walls, and adjacent to ridgelines and in gullies and bowls. The wind slabs are in isolated cases prone to triggering. Avalanches can be released, mostly by large loads.

snowpackStructure.comment: Weak layers exist in the snowpack in particular on shady slopes. The weather conditions gave rise to moistening of the snowpack, especially at low and intermediate altitudes. In general, the snow cover is gradually thinning and varies depending on altitude and exposure.

tendency.highlights: The weather will be partly cloudy.

avalancheActivity.highlights: Wind slabs and wet snow represent the main danger.
avalancheActivity.comment:
Up to 20 cm of snow, and even more in some localities, fell in the last three days above approximately 1600 m. Little snow fell during the night.
As a consequence of new snow and a sometimes moderate northeasterly wind, sometimes deep wind slabs will form by the early morning in particular adjacent to ridgelines and in gullies and bowls as well as at high altitude. In particular in the regions exposed to heavier precipitation and on very steep slopes small and medium-sized avalanches are possible. They can in isolated cases be released, even by a single winter sport participant.
As a consequence of warming during the day and solar radiation small and medium-sized moist and wet avalanches are possible below approximately 2400 m. This applies in particular in case of releases originating from steep sunny starting zones at intermediate altitudes that have retained the snow thus far. Even a small snow slide can sweep people along and give rise to falls.

snowpackStructure.comment:
Over a wide area 15 to 20 cm of snow, and up to 30 cm in some localities, fell on Tuesday above approximately 1600 m.
The new snow is lying on a crust and generally at low and intermediate altitudes.
High Alpine regions: The moderate wind has transported the new snow.
Over a wide area there is still a very large amount of snow.

avalancheActivity.highlights:
Outgoing longwave radiation during the night will be severely restricted.
Weak layers in the old snowpack necessitate defensive route selection. Shady slopes where weaknesses exist in the old snowpack are especially precarious.
As a consequence of warming during the day, the likelihood of moist avalanches being released will increase on south facing slopes below approximately 2600 m.
avalancheActivity.comment:
Weak layers in the old snowpack can still be released in some places by individual winter sport participants in particular above approximately 2400 m. This applies in particular in shady places that are protected from the wind. Avalanches can penetrate down to the ground and reach large size in isolated cases.
As a consequence of warming during the day, the likelihood of moist and wet avalanches being released will increase on south facing slopes below approximately 2600 m.
Avalanches can occur in particular in starting zones where no previous releases have taken place.

snowpackStructure.comment:
At elevated altitudes a treacherous avalanche situation will be encountered in some localities. Towards its base, the snowpack is faceted and weak. Avalanches can be released in deeper layers also.
In addition the fresh wind slabs adjacent to ridgelines and in gullies and bowls are easily triggered.
Outgoing longwave radiation during the night will be severely restricted. The surface of the snowpack cooled hardly at all during the overcast night and will already be soft in the early morning. This applies in particular on sunny slopes below approximately 2600 m.

tendency.highlights: As a consequence of warming during the day the prevalence of the avalanche prone locations will increase as the day progresses.

avalancheActivity.highlights:
Outgoing longwave radiation during the night will be severely restricted. As the moisture increases moist small and medium sized avalanches are possible.
The wind slabs can be released, especially at their margins, in particular on steep shady slopes. Weak layers in the old snowpack necessitate caution and restraint.
avalancheActivity.comment:
As a consequence of warming during the day, the likelihood of moist and wet avalanches being released will increase in particular on steep slopes below approximately 2600 m.
Dry avalanches can be released in deeper layers also.

snowpackStructure.comment:
The upper section of the snowpack is moist and its surface has a crust that is strong in many cases.
Precarious weak layers exist in the old snowpack especially on steep northwest, north and northeast facing slopes.

tendency.highlights: As a consequence of warming during the day the prevalence of the avalanche prone locations will increase as the day progresses.

avalancheActivity.highlights: Weak layers in the old snowpack necessitate caution and restraint. In some localities up to 5 cm of snow, but less in some localities, will fall above approximately 2000 m.
avalancheActivity.comment: In some localities 5 cm of snow, but less in some localities, will fall above approximately 2000 m. As a consequence of warming during the day moist avalanches are possible, but they will be mostly small. The natural activity of small avalanches will only slowly increase. Distinct weak layers in the old snowpack necessitate caution. Weak layers in the lower part of the snowpack can be released even by individual winter sport participants in particular at transitions from a shallow to a deep snowpack, when entering gullies and bowls for example. This applies in particular on northeast, north and northwest facing slopes above the tree line. The avalanches can be released in deep layers of the snowpack and reach medium size.

snowpackStructure.comment: The snowpack remains generally subject to considerable local variations. The rain gave rise to gradual moistening of the snowpack below approximately 1700 m. Above approximately 2000 m northwest, north and northeast facing slopes: Distinct weak layers in the old snowpack represent the main danger.

avalancheActivity.highlights: Weakly bonded old snow requires caution.
avalancheActivity.comment: Winter sport participants can release avalanches in isolated cases. These can reach large size in isolated cases. The avalanche prone locations are to be found in particular on very steep west, north and east facing slopes above approximately 2000 m. Caution is to be exercised in particular at transitions from a shallow to a deep snowpack, when entering gullies and bowls for example.

snowpackStructure.comment:
Some snow will fall especially in the Allgäu Alps and in the Western Lechtal Alps.

Above approximately 2000 m: Avalanche prone weak layers exist in the bottom section of the snowpack on west, north and east facing slopes. Towards its surface, the snowpack consists of faceted crystals, especially on shady slopes. In steep terrain there is a danger of falling on the hard snow surface.

Below approximately 2000 m: The snowpack will be moist.

tendency.highlights: Weakly bonded old snow requires caution. Slight increase in danger of moist avalanches as a consequence of warming during the day and solar radiation.

avalancheActivity.highlights: Wet and gliding avalanches are the main danger.

snowpackStructure.comment: In gullies and bowls above approximately 1800 m a lot of snow is lying. The spring-like weather conditions gave rise to moistening of the snowpack. These conditions brought about a gradual weakening of the snowpack also at intermediate and high altitudes. Some new snow above approximately 1800 m. Backcountry tours, off-piste skiing and ascents to alpine cabins should be started and concluded very early.

avalancheActivity.highlights: Weakly bonded old snow and wet snow require caution.
avalancheActivity.comment:
Weakly bonded old snow requires caution.
Avalanches can be released in the old snowpack, even by a single winter sport participant. They can in isolated cases release deeper layers of the snowpack and reach quite a large size. Avalanche prone locations are to be found especially above approximately 2000 m. These avalanche prone locations are to be found in particular in northwest to north to northeast facing aspects. Caution is to be exercised in particular on steep shady slopes, as well as at transitions from a shallow to a deep snowpack, when entering gullies and bowls for example.

Outgoing longwave radiation during the night was reduced. In the late morning the likelihood of slab avalanches being released will increase in particular on steep south and southwest facing slopes below the tree line.

As a consequence of warming during the day individual mostly small wet loose snow avalanches are possible.

snowpackStructure.comment:
Shady slopes: Distinct weak layers exist in the bottom section of the snowpack in particular on northwest, north and northeast facing slopes. This applies especially above approximately 2000 m.

Outgoing longwave radiation during the night was reduced. The surface of the snowpack is frozen, but not to a significant depth and will soften during the day. This applies in particular below approximately 2400 m.

The snowpack remains subject to considerable local variations over a wide area.

tendency.highlights: Outgoing longwave radiation during the night was quite good over a wide area. The danger of moist and wet avalanches will increase during the day.

avalancheActivity.comment: Only isolated dry avalanches are possible. They can be released, in particular by large loads, but they will be small in most cases. Individual avalanche prone locations are to be found on steep shady slopes at high altitude.

snowpackStructure.comment:
Shady slopes: Avalanche prone weak layers exist in the old snowpack in particular on northwest, north and northeast facing slopes.

Sunny slopes: The weather conditions gave rise to moistening of the snowpack.

avalancheActivity.comment: Above approximately 1400 m small moist and wet avalanches are possible.

avalancheActivity.highlights: Weak layers in the old snowpack can still be released in isolated cases.
avalancheActivity.comment:
Avalanches can in isolated cases be released in the old snowpack and reach medium size in isolated cases on northwest, north and northeast facing slopes. They can be released, even by a single winter sport participant.
Outgoing longwave radiation during the night was reduced. In the late morning the likelihood of slab avalanches being released will increase in particular on steep southeast and southwest facing slopes below approximately 2400 m. As a consequence of warming during the day individual mostly small wet loose snow avalanches are possible.

snowpackStructure.comment:
Avalanche prone weak layers exist in the old snowpack in particular on northwest, north and northeast facing slopes. This applies especially above the tree line.

Outgoing longwave radiation during the night was reduced. The surface of the snowpack is frozen, but not to a significant depth and will soften during the day. This applies in particular below approximately 2400 m.

The snowpack remains subject to considerable local variations over a wide area.

tendency.highlights: Outgoing longwave radiation during the night was quite good over a wide area. The danger of moist and wet avalanches will increase during the day.

avalancheActivity.highlights: Some snow will fall during the night in some regions. The backcountry touring conditions remain generally favourable.
avalancheActivity.comment:
The snowpack remains generally stable.

As a consequence of the new snow small moist snow slides are possible as the day progresses, in the event of solar radiation in particular on very steep sunny slopes.
Weak layers in the old snowpack can be released in very isolated cases. These avalanche prone locations are to be found in particular on extremely steep shady slopes above approximately 2400 m and at transitions from a shallow to a deep snowpack.

snowpackStructure.comment:
In some regions 2 to 5 cm of snow, and even more in some localities, will fall above approximately 2200 m. This snow will be deposited on surface hoar in particular on shady slopes.
Towards its surface, the snowpack is largely stable and its surface has a melt-freeze crust that is strong in many cases. In steep terrain there is a danger of falling here.
Especially steep shady slopes high altitudes and the high Alpine regions: Snow that is very compacted on the surface and stabilised by the stormy winds at the end of February, or composed of faceted crystals and, locally, snow with weak cohesion and surface frost. Weak layers exist in the old snowpack here.
Adjacent to ridgelines and in pass areas as well as at high altitude snow depths vary greatly, depending on the infuence of the wind.

tendency.highlights: The weather will be sunny. Slight increase in danger of moist and wet avalanches as a consequence of warming during the day and solar radiation. This applies on very steep sunny slopes below approximately 2700 m.

regions.name: B - VALLI DI GRESSONEY, AYAS, CHAMPORCHER

avalancheActivity.highlights: The backcountry touring conditions are mostly favourable.
avalancheActivity.comment:
In some regions up to 15 cm of snow, and even more in some localities, fell in the last three days above approximately 1800 m. The new snow is lying on top of a quite favourable old snowpack.

Weak layers in the old snowpack can be released especially by large additional loads. This applies especially on very steep shady slopes above approximately 2500 m.
As a consequence of warming during the day individual small moist and wet avalanches are possible. This applies in particular on extremely steep sunny slopes. Even a small snow slide can sweep people along and give rise to falls.

snowpackStructure.comment:
5 to 15 cm of snow fell on Tuesday above approximately 1600 m.
The old snowpack is well consolidated and its surface consists of loosely bonded snow lying on a melt-freeze crust that is strong in many cases.
Sunshine and high temperatures will give rise from the middle of the day to gradual moistening of the snowpack in particular on steep sunny slopes at intermediate altitudes.
Intermediate and high altitudes: Individual weak layers exist in the snowpack especially on northwest, north and northeast facing slopes.
In particular in the vicinity of peaks snow depths vary greatly, depending on the infuence of the wind.
